But I want to keep things simple for the user so that they can just use `iaa.<AugmenterName>`, instead of having to memorize subcategories and then use `iaa.<subcategory>.<AugmenterName>` (which also has the disadvantage that once you have defined the subcategories you can hardly change them any more, as otherwise tons of code will break). The only "clean" way that I know to achieve that and still split up the files in the project would be to "merge" them in the `__init__.py`. But that again has the disadvantage that when people want to look at the code to find out how things work exactly, they will be confused where the classes are that they import, because import statements and file structure in the repository don't match any more.
So for now I plan to just keep it this way until the files become unbearably large. Then I guess switch to the init file method, unless something better pops up.

I played around a bit with an in-memory cache using [rdflib.IOMemory](http://rdflib.readthedocs.org/en/stable/apidocs/rdflib.plugins.html#rdflib.plugins.memory.IOMemory), but the resource usage is simply too large to be practical: the loaded cache used more than 6 GB of memory on my machine.

Here's the implementation:

```diff
From ec2643e5f9017feb6f12802e31156db904777af5 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Clemens Wolff <clemens.wolff+git@gmail.com>
Date: Fri, 19 Feb 2016 16:50:59 -0800
Subject: [PATCH] Implement in-memory cache

---
 gutenberg/acquire/metadata.py | 28 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 tests/test_metadata_cache.py  |  8 ++++++++
 2 files changed, 36 insertions(+)

diff --git a/gutenberg/acquire/metadata.py b/gutenberg/acquire/metadata.py
index 565e221..251d41f 100644
--- a/gutenberg/acquire/metadata.py
+++ b/gutenberg/acquire/metadata.py
@@ -5,6 +5,7 @@
 from __future__ import absolute_import
 
 import abc
+import gzip
 import logging
 import os
 import re
@@ -210,6 +211,33 @@ class SqliteMetadataCache(MetadataCache):
         return self.cache_uri[len(self._CACHE_URI_PREFIX):]
 
 
+class InMemoryMetadataCache(MetadataCache):
+    def __init__(self, cache_location):
+        store = 'IOMemory'
+        MetadataCache.__init__(self, store, cache_location)
+        self.serialization_format = 'xml'
+
+    def populate(self):
+        MetadataCache.populate(self)
+        self._serialize()
+
+    def open(self):
+        self._deserialize()
+        self.is_open = True
+
+    def _serialize(self):
+        with gzip.open(self._local_storage_path, 'wb') as fobj:
+            self.graph.serialize(fobj, self.serialization_format)
+
+    def _deserialize(self):
+        try:
+            with gzip.open(self._local_storage_path, 'rb') as fobj:
+                self.graph = Graph()
+                self.graph.load(fobj, self.serialization_format)
+        except:
+            raise InvalidCacheException('Unable to deserialize cache')
+
+
 _METADATA_CACHE = None
 
 
diff --git a/tests/test_metadata_cache.py b/tests/test_metadata_cache.py
index 3697d9b..dcd1016 100644
--- a/tests/test_metadata_cache.py
+++ b/tests/test_metadata_cache.py
@@ -13,6 +13,7 @@ from six import u
 from gutenberg._util.url import pathname2url
 from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import CacheAlreadyExistsException
 from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import InvalidCacheException
+from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import InMemoryMetadataCache
 from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import SleepycatMetadataCache
 from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import SqliteMetadataCache
 from gutenberg.acquire.metadata import set_metadata_cache
@@ -112,6 +113,13 @@ class TestSqlite(MetadataCache, unittest.TestCase):
         self.cache.catalog_source = _sample_metadata_catalog_source()
 
 
+class TestInMemory(MetadataCache, unittest.TestCase):
+    def setUp(self):
+        self.local_storage = "%s.gz" % tempfile.mktemp()
+        self.cache = InMemoryMetadataCache(self.local_storage)
+        self.cache.catalog_source = _sample_metadata_catalog_source()
+
+
 def _sample_metadata_catalog_source():
     module = os.path.dirname(sys.modules['tests'].__file__)
     path = os.path.join(module, 'data', 'sample-rdf-files.tar.bz2')
-- 
1.9.1
```

Hi @lardconcepts I tried to reproduce your calls and I get results similar to yours (`Llandinam,UK` gives me back 11 matching items!)

This behaviour is not caused by PyOWM, it's caused by the way the API internals work. I frankly have no idea what goes on behind the scenes whenever somebody looks up data using country _names_ as a query key: I guess - but I may be wrong - that they're doing some kind of exact text matching against the locality names listed on their datastores. That's probably the reason why putting a comma or not is causing different data to be returned.

But fear not! The API mainatiners themselves admit their system is giving back some ambiguous data in some cases (like your one) and therefore [warn us to use city IDs instead of city names](https://openweathermap.org/current):

> Call API by city ID instead of city name, city coordinates or zip code. In this case you get precise respond exactly for your city. 

So here the best practice is to **avoid calling the API using strings to identify locations and use city IDs instead**

Let me be frank, this is not a bug.

The call to `weather.get_wind()` obtains a `dict` and then it's client code's responsibility to safely explore the contents of that dict.

So, this would be the best way to go:

```python
wind = weather.get_wind()
degree = wind.get('deg', None)  # then check for degree nonethiness
```

Hey @rocktavious. 

I agree that `addict` differs from the builtin `dict` class on this. The thought was to allow the second example to be written something along the lines of
```
>>> something = {"a":[1,2,{"b":[1,2,3]}]}
>>> a = Dict(something)
>>> something.a[2].b.append(4)
>>> something
{'a': [1, 2, {'b': [1, 2, 3, 4]}]}
```
But in order to enable that syntax for both lists and tuples, they are being clones such that the original list will be left intact:
```
>>> a
{'a': [1, 2, {'b': [1, 2, 3]}]}
```
I thought perhaps we could disable cloning by default, but be able to turn it on by introducing an argument that enables cloning. I'll think about it, and perhaps implement it tonight. Thanks for commenting and using! :+1: Any further thoughts?

OK I repro'd. Yes the cause is the manifest, but not because it's converting.
Schema 2 (https://github.com/docker/distribution/blob/master/docs/spec/manifest-v2-2.md) has a `config` section which is supposed to contain the container config.
`dxf` is just using the registry as a blob store, it doesn't even know about containers. But the registry wants this section.
So to give it something, instead of a `application/vnd.docker.container.image.v1+json` to describe a container config, `dxf` just gives it an `application/octet-stream` (actually, points to the first layer itself).
`docker pull` will be trying to read that file as `application/vnd.docker.container.image.v1+json` and failing.

Nm - I figured it out. It's not a bug - the weird wiggles are just an artifact of how supersampling works.

I attached a figure to illustrate. The black line is the true light curve. The blue points are the light curve sampled every 5 time units - with no supersampling. The red points ARE supersampled, with supersample_factor = 2 and exp_time = 10. In other words, each red point should be the average of the blue point on either side of it. And that's exactly what the code outputs.

The way to fix the wiggles is just to use a larger supersample_factor - as you noted.

Anyway, thanks for pointing this out! I've always had short exposure times so this has never been an issue for me.

A null terminated Char array will correctly store a UTF-8 encoded string, it cannot correctly store any other unicode string, because of the dependence on Null characters in the byte array.  In this way, a function that receives char* can handle utf-8 encode unicode without prematurely terminating the text parsing methods.  This is what UTF-8 was invented for.  Because UTF-16 would result in a early termination when parsing a null terminated string if the data was not correctly encoded.
Surely, if the data is processed in mediainfo directly, it would be very likely to cause problems.  In the case of a Mac, using utf-8, this is not the case, which is evidence that the open function merely passes the char* or wchar* off to the OS fopen() method accordingly, and is handled correctly.

This is not a statement about best practice, nor what the code ought to do, it is a statement of fact, based on observations on a system, using unicode... Including emoticons, on a filename and verifying the passing results. 

Surely, the correct solution is to use the right method for each platform, the same way MediaInfo_DLL.py does.  There is no, one solution works on all platforms.

But as i have proven already, UTF-8 is compatible with ASCII (within reason) but not the other way around.

Armand

This looks like it's failing on `OP_EQUAL_VERIFY` instruction in input/unspent `0`, the combined script of which is:

```
OP_1 OP_DUP OP_HASH160 e52b482f2faa8ecbf0db344f93c84ac908557f33 OP_EQUALVERIFY OP_CHECKSIG
                                                                        ^^^^^^
```

This is the state of the stack just before the `OP_EQUALVERIFY`:

```
OP_1 c51b66bced5e4491001bd702669770dccf440982 e52b482f2faa8ecbf0db344f93c84ac908557f33
```

`c51b66bced5e4491001bd702669770dccf440982` is the result of `hash160(b'\x01')` (note the `OP_1` as the first and only instruction to `scriptSig`). `e52b482f2faa8ecbf0db344f93c84ac908557f33` is from `scriptPubKey`.

As an aside, the combined script for input/unspent `1` looks weird to me:

```
3045022100c9cdd08798a28af9d1baf44a6c77bcc7e279f47dc487c8c899911bc48feaffcc0220503c5c50ae3998a733263c5c0f7061b483e2b56c4c41b456e7d2f5a78a74c07703 02d5c25adb51b61339d2b05315791e21bbe80ea470a49db0135720983c905aace0 OP_1
```

@derrend I've written a little send-all-the-coins script to demonstrate the high level functions.

You should read the source code of each one once you're comfortable going deeper.

```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import os
import argparse
from binascii import hexlify, unhexlify
import sys

from urllib.error import HTTPError

from pycoin.key import Key
from pycoin.tx.tx_utils import create_tx, sign_tx
from pycoin.services.blockchain_info import spendables_for_address, send_tx

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--privkey-bytes', help='provide hexlified raw privkey bytes', default='', type=str)
parser.add_argument('--send-all-to', help='where to send all the money at this address', default='1MaxKayeQg4YhFkzFz4x6NDeeNv1bwKKVA', type=str)
args = parser.parse_args()

key_bytes = unhexlify(args.privkey_bytes.encode()) if args.privkey_bytes != '' else os.urandom(32)
private_key = Key(secret_exponent=int.from_bytes(key_bytes, 'big'))
address = private_key.address()

print('Your Bitcoin address is...', address)
print('Your --privkey-bytes', hexlify(key_bytes).decode())

try:
    spendables = spendables_for_address(address)
    print('Spending', spendables)
except HTTPError as e:
    print('Blockchain throws a 500 error if there are no spendables. Try sending some coins to', address, 'and try again. Remeber to copy privkey-bytes.')
    sys.exit()

tx = create_tx(spendables, [args.send_all_to])
print('TX created:', repr(tx))

sign_tx(tx, [private_key.wif(False), private_key.wif(True)])
print('Final TX:', tx)

print('TX Send Attempt:', send_tx(tx))
```

Original function:
```python
def to_dict(self):
    vars_self = vars(self)
    bad_key_list = [x for x in vars_self.keys() if x[0] == '_']
    for key in vars_self.keys():
        if key in bad_key_list:
            del vars_self[key]
    return vars_self
```

```python
def to_dict(self):
    vars_self = vars(self)
    bad_key_list = [x for x in vars_self.keys() if x[0] == '_']
    for key in bad_key_list:
        del vars_self[key]
    return vars_self
```

I think the main difficulty is working through all the edge cases that a Markdown document can contain, and this is partly why I've been putting this issue off. For example: ```markdown **_foo_** ``` ... should be parsed as: ```html foo ``` But using the naive implementation above, we would have the output: ```markdown ***foo*** ``` ... which gets parsed as: ```html foo ``` And things get trickier when we have escape characters, which influence the parsing process, but in some cases are not reflected in the abstract syntax tree. I have some thoughts on how to get around this, but it would require some additional work apart from implementing a renderer. What are your thoughts, and what do you think would be your use case for such a renderer?

Hi @jamdagni86, I'm very sorry about not getting back to you promptly. My schedule has been very busy lately. I just had a chance to walk through the test output you provided and figured out where the main part of the issue is.

In the main `chatterbot.py` file, there is a variable called `all_statements` which is a list of every statement in the database. This list is then passed to the logic adapter which searches for the closest match to the known input and returns whatever statement the match was in response to.

The issue that you ran into was because after the first time the the similar statement was entered as input, the statement then existed in the database, however it had no response values. Your fix modifies the input statement to correct this. However this can lead to possible problems later if the input response that chatterbot provides is incorrect (very possible when working with small data sets).

To correct this issue with out modifying response data, I am going to modify the storage adapter to include a _not_ filter. This will allow the initial filter set of all known responses to exclude any statements that do not have a known response.

I am currently working on adding these changes and will create a new pull request later (likely tonight).

Again, thank you for bringing this issue to my attention, and my apologies for not getting to this sooner.

The signature for `core.get_torrent_status` can be found here: https://github.com/deluge-torrent/deluge/blob/26441693769047b460a46faf8a2440896bbd3c79/deluge/core/core.py#L601

That means you'd call it like this 

    get_torrent_status('53c17c8ab411ce3c79bc29addeb04047921d5c27', [])
That translate into 

    client.call('core.get_torrent_status', '53c17c8ab411ce3c79bc29addeb04047921d5c27', [])

The `filter_dict` is an argument for `get_torrents_status` (notice the plural torrentS) and can be used like this.

    get_torrents_status({'id': '53c17c8ab411ce3c79bc29addeb04047921d5c27'}, [])

which translates into

    client.call('core.get_torrents_status', {'id': '53c17c8ab411ce3c79bc29addeb04047921d5c27'}, [])

The syntax design is very similar to the one used in Twisted Python where function_name(args, kwargs) is translated into wrapper(function_name, args, kwargs).

The table won't refresh when the csv file is changed as it is not linked to it. Once you import to a dict, then it's new data not connected to the csv. You would have to have a method checking the contents of the csv and re-importing and refreshing the table each time.

```
import sys,os
import Tkinter as tk
from PIL import Image#, ImageTk
import time
from tkintertable.Tables import TableCanvas
from tkintertable.TableModels import TableModel
import csv

def importcsv(filename, sep=','):
    dictreader = csv.DictReader(open(filename, 'rb'), delimiter=sep)
    dictdata = {}
    count = 0
    for rec in dictreader:
        dictdata[count]=rec
        count=count+1
    return dictdata

class App(tk.Frame):
    def __init__(self):
        tk.Frame.__init__(self)
        #self.master = master
        self.screenSizeX = self.master.winfo_screenwidth()
        self.screenSizeY = self.master.winfo_screenheight()
        self.frameSizeX = int(self.screenSizeX )
        self.frameSizeY = int(self.screenSizeY )
        self.framePosX = (self.screenSizeX - self.frameSizeX)/2
        self.framePosY = (self.screenSizeY - self.frameSizeY)/2
        self.master.geometry('%sx%s+%s+%s'%(self.frameSizeX, self.frameSizeY, self.framePosX, self.framePosY))
        self.drawFrame()
        self.drawTable()

    def drawFrame(self):
        self.mainFrame = tk.Frame(self.master, height = self.screenSizeY, width = self.screenSizeX, bg = 'light green')
        self.mainFrame.pack(padx = 10, pady = 10)

        self.titleFrame = tk.Frame(self.mainFrame, height = 0.4*self.screenSizeY , width = self.screenSizeX , bg = 'light green')
        self.titleFrame.pack(padx = 10, pady = 0, side = tk.TOP)
        self.middleFrame = tk.Frame(self.mainFrame, height = 0.6*self.screenSizeY, width = self.screenSizeX, bg = 'green')
        self.middleFrame.pack(padx = 10, pady = 0)
        self.bottomFrame = tk.Frame(self.mainFrame, height = 0.1*self.screenSizeY , width = self.screenSizeX, bg = 'light green')
        self.bottomFrame.pack(padx = 10, pady = 0, side = tk.BOTTOM )

        self.mainTitle = tk.Label(self.titleFrame, text = 'bla bla bla', font = ('Helvetica', 32, 'bold'), fg='White', bg="light green")
        self.mainTitle.pack()
        self.bottomTitle = tk.Label(self.bottomFrame, text = 'bla bla bla', font = ('Helvetica', 24, 'bold'), fg='White', bg="light green")
        self.bottomTitle.grid(row=0, column=0)

    def drawTable(self):
        self.d = importcsv('query.csv')
        print self.d
        self.model = TableModel()
        self.table = TableCanvas(self.middleFrame, model=self.model, width = self.screenSizeX, thefont = ('Arial', 17),
                            height = 0.7*self.screenSizeY, rowheaderwidth=0, showkeynamesinheader=True ,rowheight=50, editable=False,
                              rowselectedcolor='White')

        self.model.importDict(self.d)

        self.model.setSortOrder(0)
        self.table.createTableFrame()
        self.table.autoResizeColumns()
        #self.table.model.columnNames = ['name','age']
        self.table.redrawTable()


def main():
    app = App()
    app.mainloop()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

        if count is not None:

Yeah, I'm running into this issue too -- it's a Python 3.x problem I believe, and `__init__.py` should be changed to use a relative import like so (note the dot):

    from .graphitesend import *

There is a `test` method which you can use to see if the current minute matches.  You would need to take care of the seconds however as the time matches the cron schedule throughout a minute:

```
print(now)                                                                      
>>> 2016-01-01 00:00:59+00:00                                                  
print(entry.previous(now))                                                      
>>> -59.0                                                                      
print(entry.test(now))                                                          
>>> True
```

I do something similar to this in a cron job monitoring application:

There's a thread which sleeps a while (5s) and then steps forward from the previous time checked (in 55s jumps) looking for minutes it didn't check yet.  (So that it doesn't miss any minutes even if the regular sleep lasts a lot longer than expected). Then it tries to match the time against the cron schedule.  It does something like this:

```Python
self.schedule = CrabSchedule('0 0 * * *', ...)
self._previous = datetime.now(pytz.UTC)

while True:
    time.sleep(5)
    delta = timedelta(seconds=55)
    current = datetime.now(pytz.UTC)
    previous = self._previous

    while minute_before(previous, current):
        previous = self._previous + delta  # (previous isn't a very good name for this)

        if not minute_equal(previous, self._previous):
            if self.schedule.match(previous):
                # Do something...

        self._previous = previous
```
The actual code is at:

https://github.com/grahambell/crab/blob/master/lib/crab/service/__init__.py#L43

https://github.com/grahambell/crab/blob/master/lib/crab/service/clean.py#L37

And it uses a class `CrabSchedule` which extends `CronTab` adding a [match](https://github.com/grahambell/crab/blob/master/lib/crab/util/schedule.py#L54) method -- I wrote that before `CronTab` provided a `test` method.

OK, I think I figured it out. 3 days ago, the robotframework-selenium2library 3.0 was officially released. That means, now if you install robotframework-extendedselenium2library it will install this version. Per the existing pull request https://github.com/rickypc/robotframework-extendedselenium2library/pull/17 ES2L is incompatible with the S2L 3.0 release. I tried to apply the changes from #17 on my system but that didn't work.

Instead, I used the following sequence to get the compatible versions installed:
```
pip install robotframework
pip install robotframework-selenium2library==1.8.0
pip install robotframework-extendedselenium2library
```
It is important to do the S2L 1.8 installation before ES2L in order to satisfy the minimum version required by ES2L yet use a compatible, older version.

`Tensor<xpu, 2> labels ` is a 2-D tensor. labels[i][j] should return the `real_t` element at position (i,j)

> It's easy to support other assign ops

Yes. The implementation of which would look quite similar to the proposed change (i.e. registering a custom function or tensor type with the parameter server). Moreover, if the operation is not actually `+=` (e.g., clipping, reweighting), then redefining assign is semantically misleading. 

> then we need to support customer merger

Yes, this is the more flexible approach. Fortunately, a casual user is not affected by the existence of a generic merge mechanism as the default remains as the C++ `+=` implementation.

@feng-tao I do not understand why does a scheduler config has to do with webserver start up time. [Webserver load dagbag in a single thread fasion](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/www/views.py#L101) as I stated in my previous comment.

You are perfectly correct that increasing # of dag file processor would help speeding up DAG file parsing. Airbnb recently bumped that up from 64 to 128 and observed improvements. However we have some limitations:

- from experimentation, on the AWS EC2 i3.16xlarge host, 256 DAG file processor would cause `Too many open files` errors and it can be limited by the computer power of the scheduler host/container.
- scheduler loop speed would be the bottleneck when we have limited # of DAG file processors, especially when we have large # of DAG files--I'll be sending out several PRs over the next quarter to scale up the scheduler.

Hi! Looks pretty good to me, just a few minor things:

1. The subject mentions "a few bugs", but I'm not sure I see any bug fixes. Can you elaborate on what you consider a bug? I just want to make sure I'm not missing anything!
2. I'm not entirely sure about the change of touch to a boolean. The specification states: "A user presence byte [1 byte]. Bit 0 is set to 1, which means that user presence was verified. (This version of the protocol doesn't specify a way to request authentication responses without requiring user presence.) A different value of Bit 0, as well as Bits 1 through 7, are reserved for future use. The values of Bit 1 through 7 should be 0". So the value is defined as a byte, but currently can only have a single value (1, since there is no way to perform an operation without requiring user presence). In that sense it actually makes more sense to do an equality check in the library itself, and raise an exception on any other value. However, the value might be changed in a future specification. For example, bits 1-7 might indicate additional information about the action performed by the user.
As these are just example files it may be overthinking it a bit here. It really doesn't matter what is returned, since it's for demonstration purposes only. Basically, if you want to return a boolean here, I think the better expression might be bool(touch & 1), ie. True iff bit 0 is set.

What specific problems are users having installing jenks? I'd anticipate two main issues:

1. they don't have `git` installed
2. they don't have a compiler toolchain to build C extension modules

Publishing an official release of the `jenks` module to pypi will solve (1) - Just `pip install jenks`. Honestly something I should do anyways. I'd much prefer to do this than fork the code and bundle it with an unrelated repo.

But (2) will still be an issue - the jenks module is implemented in Cython for speed reasons and needs a C compiler to work. This will typically affect Windows users more severly as the compiler toolchain is very easy to install on OSX and Linux. A few options to solve this 

* Build binary wheels for every target platform and python version
* Rewrite the module setup to fall back onto a (slow but working) pure python implementation in the absence of a compiler.
* Rely on another third party classification library that has these issues solved:
    - PySAL has a very robust module for handling classification with Jenks and other methods: https://pysal.readthedocs.io/en/latest/library/esda/mapclassify.html
    - There is a jenkspy module on pypi which is implemented in C but distributes windows wheels with each release. https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jenkspy

it turns out that the difference between using  `wcs_world2pix` and `all_world2pix` is strong enough that it's impossible to miss. 
So without making changes in the header, the former does the correct transformation as it does land on the target I'm interested in, whereas the other ends up in a significantly shifted position. 
But it's better that now I know what is actually going on with this.

@jamienoss - Please don't move it to a separate script. The current structure is shared between all packages in the ecosystem, and while `astropy-helpers` doesn't use the package template, the layout of the CI config files are the same as everywhere else making maintenance and automatic updates, etc. easier.

One possible way to handle this, and similar issues, might be to have two separate setup.cfg files.  The default setup.cfg would be used for source checkouts.  But when writing a source distribution it would be overwritten with a different setup.cfg for source dists.  I think Numpy has an approach somewhat like this.

@stefanv my expectation would be to use round here. If you are only doing a single transformation, the subpixel accuracy is unlikely to matter. If you are chaining many transformations through their outputs (instead of through the transformation chaining API), then you will have much bigger problems than a missing edge pixel.

The 90° rotation is a clear use case where ceil is broken. I think it should be a priority to fix it. Got any better ideas than this?

@rhyolight using python 2.7.8 installed directly from python.org, I was running into this issue.  I resolved it the same way @Lupino did, by including my python lib path in the `commonLinkFlags` as follows:
```python
  commonLinkFlags = [
    "-m" + bitness,
    "-fPIC",
    "-L" + nupicCoreReleaseDir + "/lib",
    "-L/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib", # my addition
    # for Cap'n'Proto serialization
    "-lkj",
    "-lcapnp",
    "-lcapnpc",
    # optimization (safe defaults)
    "-O2",
  ]
```

@cogmission the GroupByGenerator is an implementation detail. The thing to pay attention to is `groupByN`, which is essentially just `itertools.groupby` extended to work with multiple sequences. As long as the `groupByN` docstring is good, I think we're fine.

Found this answer from stackoverflow:
The default ModelBackend authentication backend started rejecting inactive users in Django 1.10. Therefore your authenticate() call returns None, and you get the Invalid username/password! message from the outer if/else statement.
If you use the default ModelBackend, you no longer need to check user.is_active in your login view.

If you really want authenticate to return inactive users, then you can use AllowAllUsersModelBackend instead. If you do this, then it is your responsibility to check the is_active flag in your login view.

Solved by adding authentication Backend:

``
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = ['django.contrib.auth.backends.AllowAllUsersModelBackend']
``

The main problem you are hitting here is that your `X.diff()` thing has a NaN in it:
```python
In [11]: df["X"].diff()
Out[11]: 
0   NaN
1     3
2    -4
3     3
4    -2
5     3
6    -4
7     2
Name: X, dtype: float64
```
Then when you pass that to `scale`, it tries to calculate the mean/stddev of the array, and the nan propagates and it returns an array of all-nans:
```python
In [12]: pt.builtins.scale(df["X"].diff())
Out[12]: 
0   NaN
1   NaN
2   NaN
3   NaN
4   NaN
5   NaN
6   NaN
7   NaN
Name: X, dtype: float128
```

And then patsy's missing-data handling kicks in and throws away all of these NaNs, you get back a design matrix with zero rows in it.

And then there's a bug in patsy which I should fix, where if you try to print a design matrix with zero rows then it throws an error. But that's not really your main problem, it just obscures it :-)

We need to restrict QtPy to certain versions, so I would suggest to be 5.6 compatible and make a release where we explicitly check that Qt5 is 5.6, otherwise we raise an error. Since conda packages will only support 5.6 (LTS) this seems like a safe choice for now.

Equivalently, you could [import PySide's Signal](https://github.com/spyder-ide/qtpy/blob/31dbc9eb01329af3d7d67cbfd6ee76a632aa84c2/qtpy/QtCore.py#L41):
```py
from PySide.QtCore import Signal as pyqtSignal
```
Furthermore, you could agnostically:
```py
if PYQT5:
    from PyQt5.QtCore import *
    Signal, Slot, Property = pyqtSignal, pyqtSlot, pyqtProperty
if PYSIDE:
    from PySide.QtCore import *
    pyqtSignal, pyqtSlot, pyqtProperty = Signal, Slot, Property
``` 
and avoid tensions to force the user to write their software in a certain way.

For anyone still trying to achieve this effect with Font Awesome (5.0.13 as of this comment) working with JS and SVG, @ccprog 's solution above worked for me. 

I had several icons that I wanted to apply a gradient to; I didn't care about hover, I just wanted it on there.

Here are the steps I followed to achieve this effect:

1. I used [Angry Tools](http://angrytools.com/gradient/)'s gradient generator to give me an svg I can define.
2. Define an svg element in your html. 
```html
<svg width="0" height="0">
	<linearGradient id="lgrad" x1="100%" y1="100%" x2="0%" y2="0%" >
		<stop offset="0%" style="stop-color:rgb(252,207,49);stop-opacity:1" />
		<stop offset="100%" style="stop-color:rgb(245,85,85);stop-opacity:1" />
	</linearGradient>
</svg>
```
3. In your stylesheet, reference the svg icons you would like to apply the gradient to. *With Font Awesome 5.0.13, using js, your `<i>` tags are replaced with inline-svg.*
```css
.parent-div svg * {
  fill: url(#lgrad);
}
```

**Done!**

See [this JSFiddle](https://jsfiddle.net/alexanderlee58/fgxuLotv/) for an example.

I struggled getting a grasp on this issue, but I think I we can make something work.

### Status Quo

We simply _don't do human format_ for workflow schema commands. We force-switch the runtime format to JSON. Human format will not work because it expects a resource with columns, and this doesn't fit that pattern.

### Proposal

Printing the "changed" status implies going through the human formatting code. That means that we will need a special clause in the human formatting method to echo the contents in YAML (or in the future, perhaps a fancy graphical view).

Similar to other commands, this _also_ implies that the "changed" key will be present for YAML or JSON output, example:

```yaml
- job_template: 12
- job_template: 12
- job_template: 13
  success_nodes:
  - failure_nodes:
    - job_template: 14
    job_template: 15
  - failure_nodes:
    - job_template: 14
    job_template: 12
- changed: True
```

Of course, the changed status should be smart as to whether any unsafe methods (POST, DELETE, etc.) were used in our new fancy idempotent algorithm.

Actually Feike is just using dcs.client object, which is not necessary available for different DCS implementations.
So far it's available for  Etcd and ZooKeeper and this client object even has similar interfaces of `delete` method:
```python
KazooClient.delete(self, path, version=-1, recursive=False)
etcd.Client.delete(self, key, recursive=None, dir=None, **kwdargs)
```

So recursive `delete` for ZooKeeper will work as well. But for example it will not work for Consul.
Instead of doing this ugly hack, one should add the new abstract method to the AbstractDCS class, something like `delete_cluster` and implement it in Etcd and ZooKeeper classes.

The trick is to write the test in such way that they WILL be able to run in parallel on Travis. 
We cannot wait for them to run sequentially for two reasons: feedback loop will be to long and 2nd that Travis has a timeout for job execution.

The Travis-after-all was added there in order to deal with this and getting rid of it will not sort the problem. We need to write the tests in such way that they will not fail even if 3-4 of them are running in parallel. That's why we do use project names that include each python version and the current username, so the operations executed by one test session will not affect another session.

It already handles that, via the following in its own `setup.py`:

Note that it doesn't explicitly support Python 3.3 or 3.4.

```python
try:
    setup(**setup_dict)

    # the pure Python version.

Let's first agree in principle on the purpose of having a compatibility library such as the HBase client for Java and the HappyBase client for Python:

* our goal is to be compatible with code that uses HBase — this gets us instant integrations that work with HBase and user code that works with HBase becomes trivially portable
* as such, we should aim to have Bigtable become a drop-in replacement for HBase: code that works with HBase should be working with Bigtable with a tiny amount of configuration changes + code to connect to Bigtable
* we should avoid having to tell users to special-case Bigtable in their code that works with HBase, it should be transparent to the code as to whether it's working with HBase or Bigtable
* in essence, our library should *hide* the differences between HBase and Bigtable, and thus absorb complexity where possible, rather than exposing the differences and forcing customers to write 2 backend implementations, one for HBase and a separate one for Bigtable

Assuming we agree on the above, I think this argues that we should **not** throw exceptions, but either implement what we need to do, and make unnecessary functions no-ops whenever possible. Of course, each function/method should be considered on its own merits, what its intended semantics are, etc., but the goal here is not to reimplement the HBase _semantics_ 1:1, but make it easy for code to use HBase and Bigtable transparently, with little to no work from the user.

AIUI, in HBase, before deleting a table or modifying some settings, you have to first disable it. Thus, all HBase-compliant code will *always* call `disable_table()` prior to calling `delete_table()`.

I would argue that in this case, we should:

* convert calls to `disable_table()` and `enable_table()` to be no-ops rather than throw exceptions
* e.g., always return true to `is_enabled()`
* drop the warning from `drop_table()` being called with `True` for dropping table first
* document the difference that Bigtable tables have no concept of `disabled` state, because we can modify settings and delete tables without the prerequisite of disabling first

Note that this means that we would fail a test of the following type:

```python
...
table.enable()
assertTrue(table.is_enabled())  # succeeds
table.disable()
assertFalse(table.is_enabled())  # fails
```

but I would argue this is a corner case and not something anyone should be doing in practice, outside of HBase tests themselves.

Other functions/methods should be considered on a case-by-case basis.

Let's consider the opposite: what do we gain from throwing exceptions? I am not sure.

What is the side effect of throwing exceptions?

Typically, this means that portability is hindered from HBase to Bigtable, leading to several options:

* users will either give up, or
* if they want to persevere, they will write abstraction libraries that separate HBase functions from Bigtable functions, or
* users fork gcloud-python and are unable to upgrade since they made local modifications

None of these are good outcomes, from my perspective, as they are providing a sub-par user experience, and we would be the cause of all of them.

Thus, I would advocate that we try to adapt our API to be _functionally_ matching the intended semantics of HappyBase rather than trying to implement them _strictly_ and throw exceptions where we are incompatible, because that breaks the spirit of the point of having a compatibility library in the first place.

Thoughts?

@seanmt13 actually if that's your code it does reveal the underlying issue: You need to wait on the subscription future to receive notifications when unrecoverable errors occur:

```python
def main():
    #Receives messages from a pull subscription.
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(project, subscription_name)

    def callback(message):
        log.info('Received message: {}'.format(message))
        try:
            ret = bl.process_message(message)
            if ret.status_code == 200:
                log.info('200 File processed')
                statsd.increment('data-log-file-processed')
                message.ack()
            else:
                log.error('Could not process message for data log file publisher. {}' .format(ret.message))
                message.ack()
        except Exception as e:
            log.error('Could not process message: {} error occurred. {}' .format(ret.message, e))
    future = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path, callback=callback)

    # The subscriber is non-blocking, so we must keep the main thread from
    # exiting to allow it to process messages in the background.
    log.info('Listening for messages on {}'.format(subscription_path))
    
    # Wait for the result on the future. If an unrecoverable error occurs, this will raise.
    future.result()
```

> Is this only supported through AWS::Serverless::Function?

What you are referring to is only supported on the Function because it is overriding a Resource/Path/Method. There is no concept of this at the API level, so we only expose a `DefaultAuthorizer` which gets applied to all paths/methods.

The thing with `!Ref` on an Authorizer is it's not really a `!Ref`. That is, because these are constructed using Swagger and not CloudFormation resources, you can't use `!Ref MyAuthorizer` or `!GetAtt MyAuthorizer.Arn` throughout the rest of your template. This is an implementation detail that most SAM users don't need to understand, but it is also a technical limitation and may lead to more confusion if we were to define it as a `Resource` yet you can't access it in the same way you reference other resources.

TLDR: While `Authorizer: MyCognitoAuth` seems strange/confusing, I think it would be more confusing if customers try to use `!Ref MyAuthorizer` or `!GetAtt MyAuthorizer.Arn` and get unexpected results.

@HuangKBAaron , below is a function that I gathered up from other sources and created a few elements that I have used in a recent project.  This creates a header and footer on every page in the document.  I do not know how to put them on just the first page, but you can figure that out I would like to know.  Hopefully this helps you in your situation while the header and footer feature is integrated into python-docx.  Thanks for your hard work @scanny and @eupharis .

**Note**: For some reason the function won't copy into a single code block so it is split into two separate ones and will probably need to be copied one at a time.

`def write_header_and_footer(header_text, footer_text):`

    win32gencache = win32client.gencache

    if win32gencache.is_readonly == True:
        #allow gencache to create the cached wrapper objects
        win32gencache.is_readonly = False
        
        #under py2exe the call in gencache to __init__() does not happen
        #so we use Rebuild() to force the creation of the gen_py folder
        win32gencache.Rebuild()
        
        #Ensures that the gen_py directory is created before use
        win32gencache.GetGeneratePath()

        #You must ensure that the python...\win32com.client.gen_py dir does not exist
        #to allow creation of the cache in %temp%

    #Ensures that the MS Word object is created and ready for use
    win32gencache.EnsureModule('{00020905-0000-0000-C000-000000000046}', 0, 8, 5)

    word = win32gencache.EnsureDispatch('Word.Application')
    doc = word.Documents.Open(wordDocument)
    word.Visible = False
    activeDocument = word.ActiveDocument.Sections(1)
    activeDocument.Headers(win32client.constants.wdHeaderFooterPrimary).Range.Text = header_text
    activeDocument.Headers(1).PageNumbers.Add(2, True)
    activeDocument.Headers(1).PageNumbers.NumberStyle = 8
    activeDocument.Footers(win32client.constants.wdHeaderFooterPrimary).Range.Text = footer_text
    word.ActiveDocument.Save()
    doc.Close(True)
    word.Application.Quit()

XGBoost demo using the [Audit](https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/blob/master/src/test/resources/csv/Audit.csv) dataset:
```Python
import pandas

df = pandas.read_csv("Audit.csv")

df["Deductions"] = df["Deductions"].replace(True, "TRUE").replace(False, "FALSE").astype(str)
df["Adjusted"] = df["Adjusted"].astype(int)

from sklearn2pmml import sklearn2pmml, PMMLPipeline
from sklearn2pmml.decoration import CategoricalDomain
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelBinarizer
from sklearn_pandas import DataFrameMapper
from xgboost.sklearn import XGBClassifier

mapper = DataFrameMapper([
	("Occupation", [CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_is"), LabelBinarizer()]),
	("Education", [CategoricalDomain(invalid_value_treatment = "as_is"), LabelBinarizer()])
])
classifier = XGBClassifier()

pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
	("mapper", mapper),
	("classifier", classifier)
])
pipeline.fit(df, df["Adjusted"])

sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "pipeline.pmml", with_repr = True)
```

If the resulting `pipeline.pmml` file is opened in text editor, then the following `/PMML/MiningModel/MiningSchema` declaration can be seen:
```xml
<MiningSchema>
	<MiningField name="Adjusted" usageType="target"/>
	<MiningField name="Education" missingValueTreatment="asIs" invalidValueTreatment="asIs"/>
	<MiningField name="Occupation" missingValueTreatment="asIs" invalidValueTreatment="asIs"/>
</MiningSchema>
```

For your second point, these tokens have special meaning that the _model_ needs to know about.  The model doesn't currently have access to the `DatasetReader` object, and it isn't really desirable from an API standpoint to give it.  So instead we define shared constants that both the `DatasetReader` and the `Model` use.  It doesn't really make sense to configure this in the tokenizer.  We had some discussion of this here: https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/pull/366#discussion_r143308089.

Yes, we have an option to use the test set to compute the vocabulary.  This is so that you can use glove embeddings for words that show up only in the test set.  This isn't cheating, because words that are added this way are never seen at training time and thus never get trained - you're not getting any signal from the test set.

@mcarans 
On table level we support `skip_rows` argument. So we could use it like this to skip second rows of a data source:

```python
table = Table(data, schema=schmea, skip_rows=[2])
```

It's not a part of the Data Package standard for now but as a concrete implementation `datapackage-py` still could add support for it:

```python
package = Package({'resources': [{'name': 'name', 'path': 'path', skipRows: [2]]})
```

@vitorbaptista 
There was a long discussion on path security in Specs Working Group that has been finished with this resolution (http://specs.frictionlessdata.io/data-resource/#path-data-in-files):
> POSIX paths (unix-style with / as separator) are supported for referencing local files, with the security restraint that they MUST be relative siblings or children of the descriptor. Absolute paths (/) and relative parent paths (../) MUST NOT be used, and implementations SHOULD NOT support these path types.

> / (absolute path) and ../ (relative parent path) are forbidden to avoid security vulnerabilities when implementing data package tools. These limitations on resource path ensure that resource paths only point to files within the data package directory and its subdirectories. This prevents data package tools being exploited by a malicious user to gain unintended access to sensitive information. For example, suppose a data package hosting service stores packages on disk and allows access via an API. A malicious user uploads a data package with a resource path like /etc/passwd. The user then requests the data for that resource and the server naively opens /etc/passwd and returns that data to the caller.

So for now a valid data package descriptor can't have path `/tmp/my-data.csv`. And I think it's really good - to protect all users by default. But as an implementation we could provide an additional functionality like a flag to support unsafe paths. Something like this:

```python
package = Package(descriptor, safe=False)
```
And disable this `is_safe_path` checks for this case.

> InstanceType
> The instance type, such as t2.micro. The default type is "m1.small". For a list of instance types, see Instance Families and Types.
> Required: No
> Type: String
> Update requires: Some interruptions for Amazon EBS-backed instances
> Update requires: Replacement for instance store-backed instances


See http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-ec2-instance.html#cfn-ec2-instance-instancetype

Today I encountered this for the second time. First time I just restarted the builder and that solved it, but this time I wanted to get to the bottom of things. Together with the deis maintainers, we were able to find the root cause of the issue, and I have a pull request on the way to solve it.

What happened was that some folders and files were suddenly owned by root:
```
drwxrwxr-x 1 git  git  132 2015-01-07 17:55:42.795319423 +0000 .
drwxrwxr-x 1 git  git  140 2015-01-07 17:29:50.211447886 +0000 ..
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root  76 2015-01-07 17:29:49.036462354 +0000 16
drwxrwxr-x 1 git  git   76 2015-01-07 17:23:04.257444444 +0000 17
drwxrwxr-x 1 git  git   76 2015-01-07 17:23:04.245444592 +0000 25
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root  76 2015-01-07 17:29:49.037462342 +0000 33
```

This caused a permission error.

The reason why it happened was due to executing `git gc` as root:
https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/18cf1a99d333bde630f1bd3bad8029752d1799a9/builder/image/templates/builder#L179?

The [builder script](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/18cf1a99d333bde630f1bd3bad8029752d1799a9/builder/image/templates/builder) was [executed as root](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/18cf1a99d333bde630f1bd3bad8029752d1799a9/builder/image/templates/gitreceive#L56-L57) because Deis needed root permissions to execute docker commands. Root permissions is not necessary if we add the `git` user to the `docker` group (See: [Giving non-root access](https://docs.docker.com/installation/ubuntulinux/#giving-non-root-access)).

Pull request is on the way!

Trying to reason about this....

When doing a full block import we need to re-execute the block and verify that the final resulting block is identical to the input block.  Optimization here includes skipping rebuilding the receipt and transaction tries.

When incrementally building a block we need to execute the transaction **and** build the transaction and receipt tries.

This seems like we should be able to fix this as follows by separating *concerns* to the appropriate level.

- `Chain.apply_transaction()`
  - delegates to `VM.apply_transaction()`
- `VM.apply_transaction()`
  - delegates to `State.apply_transaction()`
  - updates local `VM.block.header.state_root`
  - updates local `VM.block.transactions`
  - updates local `VM.block.header.transactions_root`
  - updates local `VM.block.header.receipts_root`
  - updates local `VM.block.header.bloom`
  - updates local `VM.block.header.gas_used`
- `State.apply_transaction()`
  - executes the transaction
  - returns the `computations, receipt, state_root`  (modified from previously returning `block`.

This isn't exactly right, but the idea is to move *more* of the  block stuff up to the VM level so that `import_block` can strictly use the `State` level functions to shortcut the overhead of rebuilding each of these block/header values after each transaction is executed.

@pipermerriam I started working on #561 but I think I hit a bit of a conceptional road block.

In the old model of the `JournalDB` we could do the following:

1. Do changes
2. Take `Snapshot 1`
3. Do changes
4. Take `Snapshot 2`
3. Do changes
4. Take `Snapshot 3`
5. Commit `Snapshot 3` (merged `Snapshot 3` into `Snapshot 2`
6. Revert to `Snapshot 1`

Now the underlying DB would be rolled back to the exact state of `Snapshot 1`. I added a [test case](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/563/commits/bbe63061994844e6a62444aa79893b00e8302655) for that to demonstrate things with code.

In the new proposed model, if I'm not mistaken, we can revert back and forth between different snapshots but at the point where we commit `Snapshot 3` there's no turning back to `Snapshot 1` anymore.

This is because Snapshots (as I understood your proposal) do not track the original values anymore but rather track the *changes* (which we write to the underlying db only when commited).

Of course we could say that when we commit `Snapshot 3` we are basically throwing away all previous snapshots and start over but while that keeps the system sound it still means we are losing functionality that was previously perfectly fine.

Notice that the PR is still WIP and the `CachedJournalDB`  does not even write back to the underlying db yet. I just stumbled over this and wanted to reassure if I'm on the right track.

> @OddBloke, might I ask for your use case? Trying to learn how people are using wagon.

We have a tool which registers the Ubuntu cloud images in to each cloud.
The tool gets updated often enough that having a single shared
installation on our workers wouldn't be practical, so we install it
afresh per-run.  The runs can be highly parallel, and we depend
(transitively) on a number of packages which require compilation at
install-time, so installation is very IO and CPU heavy.  Creating binary
wheels once is obvious solution to this, and wagon makes that very easy.

(It also allows us to have a single artifact that we can move through a
CI pipeline, though our CI is still fairly nascent.)

That's already possible.

It's remarkably simple to duplicate the filters that selected the source index (provided you did that) in the very next action and delete the index or indices you selected.

```
---
actions:
  1: 
    action: reindex
    options:
      continue_if_exception: False
      wait_interval: 9
      max_wait: -1
      request_body:
        source:
          index: REINDEX_SELECTION
        dest:
          index: logs-2017.04   
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: logs-
    - filtertype: period
      source: creation_date
      range_from: -1
      range_to: -1
      unit: months
  2:
    action: delete_indices
    options:
      continue_if_exception: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: prefix
      value: logs-
    - filtertype: period
      source: creation_date
      range_from: -1
      range_to: -1
      unit: months
```

 With `continue_if_exception` set to `False` (which is the default), it will not continue and delete anything unless the reindex is successful.

Blobxfer does not upload blobs as one operation if they are under 64MB unless the file is zero-bytes. Files with size greater than zero bytes are chunked into blocks and uploaded in parallel. From what I can gather here, it looks like block uploading requires the Write permission.

blobxfer 1.0.0a1 will include a `--no-overwrite` flag which will protect you from overwriting blobs on the remote side independent of SAS.

I have a general comment about the MR regarding how it implements the `RequestLifecycle` on top of the tracing system. Remember that the `aiohttp.tracing.Trace` class is an internal class that is not directly used by the client, so you have almost full freedom to change it.

Why am I saying this?

In this MR the `RequestLifecycle` behaves as a proxy of the `Trace` class implementing for that different strategies such as the `SIGNALS` inverted dictionary or the runtime injection of signals via `setattr`. IMHO they are weak, implicit and might be a prone error system.

My suggestion here would be, get rid of the `Trace` class and implement all of the signals methods as explicit ones for the `RequestLifecycle` class, it should help you in at least the following things:

1) Get rid of the _tm function the start
2) Get rid of the SIGNALS inverted index
3) Get rid of the `setattr`.
4) Call the listeners explicitly, if they are, in the methods implemented in the `RequestLifecycle`.

What do you think? @asvetlov ?

Also, have in mind that with this change we are forcing to call the `lifecycle.send_X` specific function at each specific part of the code instead of saving some python operations - calling is hard - checking if there are traces configured. Everybody is fine with this?

> Once again, I am not a security researcher. When something says "removing old insecure certs" I go "Yay!" I don't imagine it breaking things like amazon.com.

You don't need to be a security researcher to collect Mozilla's trusted certificate bundle and package it for Python (which is `<=`80% of this project). (By the way, Mozilla removed these almost a year ago if memory serves right. Requests first found this in November and the change was months old by then. All distros I found trust Mozilla's bundle.)

> Did that May release ever happen? As of this morning I was running 2015.4.28 which

>  I used to rely on certifi and not worry about breaking changes. Now it seems like I have to worry, and the next time it is going to break things this way I'd like to have some warning so that I can test appropriately.

This is the tragedy of open source. Progress sometimes (arguably often) requires breaking things. Most users don't pay close enough attention to their projects development and most projects don't have adequate ways of telling users that something is coming down the pike. Projects that use DeprecationWarnings either get tons of flak (see my earlier post describing in brief the hate mail we received over issuing warnings in requests) or people don't see it because they're not paying attention to test runs and on Python 2.7 all warnings are ignored unless you're running tests. **tl;dr** There is **no** good way to warn users. All users want to know when this is happening and that's impossible. If you'd like to work on a way to fix that, great, let's discuss that elsewhere.

The main difference between logging and print is that each logging call seems to call flush, which triggers the actual send of IOPub messages, whereas print buffers data to be sent less frequently. In a case like yours, there is typically only one or two flushes for many fast print statements. While the code in logging runs a bit slower (~20ms vs 8ms for me), the crux is definitely the fact that you mainly see the difference when shift-entering through the code, rather than Run All.

Run All submits all the cells for execution at once, the relevant effect of which is that all execute requests have left the browser by the time output starts to arrive. When you shift-enter through cells, your clicking speed gives the outputs from the previous cell a chance to start arriving. The congestion caused by many outputs arriving while you are trying to send a new request is the cause of the delay - it's all in the UI. You will see the same thing if you add `sys.stdout.flush()` after each `print`.

So the general issue here is: handling many small stream outputs in the notebook is slow.

Notebooks are always spawned by web request in the current notebook application. It depends a bit on what you mean by "spawn", though.

Right now, when you click a link in the tree view, it navigates to `/notebooks/notebook-name.ipynb`. The javascript on this page then makes a POST request to `/api/services` to open a session and get a kernel for that notebook.

You can do either or both of these from your own webpage, but you may need to do some CORS configuration if your server and the notebook server are on different domains.

I think there's a couple of different things going on there:

1. Backspace processing (`fixBackspace()` in `notebook/static/base/js/utils.js`) isn't correctly handling consecutive backspaces when they arrive together. That should be fixable.
2. In the terminal, backspace moves the cursor back, and printing another character overwrites it. We're not emulating a proper terminal, so we don't track a cursor position, but we support a limited subset of terminal-like behaviour by processing some control characters and escape sequences. I think that in this scope, treating backspace as 'delete the last character' is 'good enough'.

> Is this 2 weeks BEFORE the expiration date? If so, this would be counter-intuitive as people would generally expect the names to stop working on the expiration date/block. It would be better if there was a 2 week or longer grace/redemption period AFTER expiration where the user can still renew the name before it becomes available for anyone to register again.

Version 0.17 (hard fork of 0.17) adds a 5000-block name expiration grace period, whereby the owner can send a `NAME_RENEWAL` transaction but do nothing else.  The name will not resolve during the grace period.

@abalkin Normally yes, and certainly UTC and tzoffset will remain immutable, but one potential race condition even for the immutable classes (though I'm guessing with minor effects) at the creation of the initial object between checking if you need to create a new object and actually creating one.

For `tzutc` I *think* this won't be a problem because the first constructor call happens at import time when creating the `UTC` constant. For `tzoffset` I'm using `setdefault`, so I think the GIL will take care of the races there. I think a similar strategy can be employed for other `tzinfo` subclasses.

The bigger race condition problem I think will be if the more complex classes like `tzfile` are memoized. There's already a cache for `tzicalvtz` and I intend to add lru-style caches for the other time zone subclasses. The implementation there is more likely to have thread-safety problems (though, I imagine, with little consequence), and that would just be compounded if there were a single cache (as opposed to separate instances that may have been created on separate threads).

I don't think it's a real problem, just something to consider in the implementation.

So lets break it into pieces to make it easier to discuss.

- We had 30 tests and the are all [passing](https://travis-ci.org/python-visualization/folium/jobs/75993480#L359) in the new framework :tada:

- Inside `travis.yml` and `setup.py` I am reading the requirements from `requirements.txt`.  In the future I want to have `requirements-extras.txt` (with: pandas, vincent, geopandas, etc), and `requirements-dev.txt` (the testing stuff: mock and pytest). Out of a desire to not make this PR any longer, I added a [wiki page called "Cell Model Solutions](../wiki/Cell-Model-Solutions) to discuss this further.

Specifically I wanted to address @cwhanse comments:

>ivcurve_npts specified. In this case, pvlib gets to choose the voltage points. We are OK with brentq here, or any of the other methods

IMO we would use `bishop88()` here, as long as the user does **not** specify the actual cell voltages, this is the fastest, safest method. It is explicit, and does not require any iterative method.

>a vector V (or I) is provided. In this case I don't see how brentq (or any other method working with the diode voltage) is an option.

IMO we would use either `brentq`, `newton`, or `lambertw`. Bishop's method isn't used here, but since `bishop88()` is just another representation of the SDM, it works just fine as a set of governing equations. Actually `brentq` or any bisection method would be considered the "gold" method, because it is _guaranteed_ to converge for any specific cell voltage in quadrant 1.

>Does this leave us with lambertw, bisection and custom newton?

No, you can use either bisection, `brentq`, `newton`, or `lambertw`. Note, "Brent" is just another way of saying "bisection", and `brentq` literally stands for "Brent" + "quadratic extrapolation" - it's just a combination of bisection and other faster methods to get close to the answer quickly. It's considered the better than `newton` and the best of the scalar zero search functions because it's bounded and is not limited by the size of the Newton step.

>I'm not in favor of custom newton unless it's really an advantage, for the reason that I'd rather focus effort on our application rather than to maintain/improve the guts of a well-known numerical method.

I agree completely. I think we should use off the shelf code, copy&pasted from SciPy, if needed. I also think we should continue to politely seek a review of our [vectorized newton PR](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/8357), and try to resolve any conflicts necessary so that it can be merged.

One idea that I haven't tested well yet, is to use a multivariate solver, like `hybrj` with sparse matrices and set the diagonal of the jacobian to the derivatives. If we just use the secant version, `hybrd` then it should  just work out of the box with no modifications. These solvers are both already part of SciPy in the [`root(method=hybr)`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/optimize.root-hybr.html#optimize-root-hybr) and [`fsolve`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.fsolve.html) methods. These methods should be very fast because they are written in FORTRAN and optimized by the compiler, and will solve for all of the input conditions simultaneously.

This `cyclesim` and `simulator` are legacy applications  (much like `interop2csv`) that were written early in the development of the library, but we never got around to removing or updating them.

We could add additional parameters to this one to further filter by those conditions.

The one caveat is that you would need to update the RunInfo.xml yourself at the moment.

I understand that recursion/quantification is hard to deal with. But I'm curious if one particular use case can be supported: When the control path is completely concrete. In this example, the recursive calls `(computeSize 1 6)` are not only ground, their evaluation can be completely unrolled since the control-path is all concrete. It may be hard to detect this in general of course, but perhaps certain cases can be supported.

The underlying issue here isn't really that users need to reason about recursion really, but rather SMTLib simply has no way of writing a simple for-loop. So, it's just the programming aspect here to be able to express benchmarks that can be completely unrolled, instead of actually doing any fancy reasoning about recursion.

I understand this is hand-wavy, but I'm curious if there might be a middle ground that supports the programming aspects of specifications, without being terribly hard to implement.

I can see that it's a pretty serious problem if you're running iZ3 under java. Unfortunately, the method iz3translate_main that's causing the problem has a pretty complicated recursion pattern with about a dozen recursive calls. If you tried to refactor this into an iterative routine, you'd almost certainly introduce subtle bugs (subtle because some of the cases occur rarely and are very difficult to stimulate). I'm going to mark this issue closed, as I think it's impractical to fix.

Sorry if that's offtopic but we've got a Stack Augmentation for custom recurrent unit with full lasagne compatibility.

The stack (a MUCH simpler version of the same NTM idea) a potential to implement the NTM if necessary - that would be just about plugging in more math.

The original article on Stack RNNs - http://arxiv.org/abs/1503.01007

Here's an example on how it works on a problem described in the original article:
[Stack-augmented GRU](https://github.com/yandexdataschool/AgentNet/blob/master/examples/Stack%20RNN%20for%20formal%20sequence%20modelling.ipynb
)

We tried to apply it to a range of semi-practical tasks, but the only place it was useful at was "linux source language model" from [Karpathy's example](http://karpathy.github.io/2015/05/21/rnn-effectiveness/) where stacks helped closing brackets, etc. - as an addition to a large GRU flotilla.

Generating linux sources isn't that practical, but i believe these augmentations may be useful when working with any highly formalized data, like code sources, html, xml, json, tex, etc. Some "ads detection from HTML"/"malware code detection"-like problems may be worth trying, that is just a humble opinion of mine. 

My personal gut feeling is that NTM won't have much more luck with practical usage "as is".

I believe that the vanilla NTM would just be a higher quantity of that "math translation" plus a few tricks.

> Since this is still an open issue, I started gathering more information about it.

Thank you, and sorry for the late reply! Haven't had a chance to look before.

> Looking in appendix A of the original article (https://arxiv.org/abs/1406.4729), the authors seem to have implemented this with a mix of different strides and pooling sizes.

I see, thank you for the pointer.

> The SPP layer with default pooling regions (1,2,4) returns a different output size if either the height or width of the incoming layer is 6, 7 or 11 (I checked all input sizes ranging from 4x4 to 1000x1000, and these were the only problematic cases)

It's easy to just generate ~all~ many problematic cases at once:
```python
inp_size = np.arange(100)
pool_dim = np.arange(50)[:,np.newaxis] + 1
win_size = (i + pool_dim - 1) // pool_dim
str_size = i // pool_dim
pad = 0
out_size = i + 2*pad - win_size + 1
wrong = out_size != pool_dim
```
Now `wrong[p,i]` says whether the output size is wrong for input size `i` and number of pooling bins `p+1`.

> The padding solution also seems reasonable to me,

It doesn't seem so easy, unfortunately. Note that there are many more broken cases for larger numbers of pooling bins, such as 6:
```
xxxxxxxxx
ww
 ww
  ww
   ww
    ww
     ww
      ww
       ww
```
For 9 inputs, the current implementation gives 8 instead of 6 output locations. We could pad this to 12 inputs and increase the stride to 2, but then one of the windows would just be two zeros. We could also crop it to 7 inputs and keep the stride at 1, but then we'd loose some data. Both is a bit unsatisfactory.

> what do you think about this strategy?

It's nice that it's still reasonably fast, and that it's following the paper. I think we shouldn't replace the default implementation with this, both for backwards compatibility and for performance reasons, but we could add a `mode` argument to the layer to control which implementation to choose. This one could be called `mode='kaiming'`.

Another aspect is that your implementation does not require cuDNN. The bleeding-edge version of Theano also doesn't require cuDNN for pooling with symbolic stride and window size. We could add `lasagne.layers.pool.SpatialPyramidPoolingLayer`, with an implementation compatible to `SpatialPyramidPoolingDNNLayer`, and a `mode` keyword for the alternative implementation. Fixing the default implementation would have to be deferred until we have a bright idea :)

Thus based on Python MRO, whoever is put as the first in your class definition gets to use its template. 

Solution would be to write your own change list template and sets its path into the attribute `change_list_template` in your `PartsAdmin` class.

Along the lines of a dedicated object here, what if there were a wrapper type, like this:

```python
u = URL()

The two problems I see here are:

I just got bitten by this too. As previously quoted, the spec says (emphasis mine):

>  If the client is paginating with `first`/`after`, then the client **may** return _true_ if edges prior to after exist, if it can do so efficiently, otherwise **may** return _false_.

By my reading, the current behavior is _technically_ within spec because the behavior is prescribed as "may" and not "must". That said, I suspect that like me, it's very counter-intuitive to consumers, and it cost quite a bit of time and debugging to figure out. 

My sense is that this specific PR would dramatically improve the greenfield developer experience, however, I can see how it would likely break existing usages, especially considering that the default value for an unspecified `slice_start` param is `0`. This behavior makes sense for the case where the slice itself begins in the correct place, and where it is not possible to efficiently determine what came before it. 

Is there some way this could be reworked to accommodate both cases? For instance what about an optional boolean param that indicates whether the `slice_start`, `list_length`, and `list_slice_length` are known to be correct with respect to the entire dataset? (i.e. `start_lengths_valid` or something) That seems like it could avoid breaking existing consumers while _dramatically_ reducing confusion for situations where this info is available and known to be valid (which I suspect would include many, many situations backed by traditional relational databases).

In theory, main CFN Sphere yaml file sets are the same across all environments with the difference being the parameters such as instance size, CIDR block definitions etc. Without dynamically generating the yaml the easiest way is to be able set the correct parameters in environment variables (and store them in files) per environment. This way a change to a parameter does not require change to the CFN Sphere YAML.

For X, the library maintains a means of mapping characters to keycodes and
modified that is initially retrieved by reading the layout.

Every time it is requested to handle a key with a graphic representation,
it searches for the corresponding keycode-modifier pair and sends an event.
If the character cannot be found, it locates an unused character in the
layout, adds the requested glyph at that position and sends the new pair.
That is the reason it can be used to type for example Russian or Thai even
though no characters of those alphabets are mapped in the layout.

X can barely be seen under all its warts.

@zballs @mgrand I did want to point out another potential cause that relates to the error message in the title.

Usually, if you get back a 400 with a payload that resembles

```json
{
  "message": "The browser (or proxy) sent a request that this server could not understand."
}
```

It's because of bad headers or something else mal-formed about your HTTP request. If you see this error, then the BigchainDB node hasn't actually looked at the payload or tried to validate it; this is a pretty typical Flask (our webserver) error that gets spit out if something looks weird to it.

For example, I often get this error if I try to do a GET with `Content-Type` instead of `Accept` headers.

-----------------

If the transaction payload is actually invalid, a different message saying that should come back.

> If you can include motivation behind your thoughts that would be useful

Asynchronous Hyperband is part of my motivation but not all of it. This is an adaptive training function, one that decides to train models based on prior evaluations and stored metadata. Wikipedia has [a good example][1] of the model updating after seeing one evaluation, what I'm used to seeing in adaptive algorithms.

> run it [update] only after all of the previous instructions finish.

This won't perform well for synchronous Hyperband. There are a couple embarrassingly parallel loops in synchronous Hyperband, and there's no reason for loop to wait on another. It will work well for one successive halving call in synchronous Hyperband.

This is important: the initial call to `update` would return anywhere between 1 and 81 calls to `_partial_fit`. This doesn't really go away in later calls to `update`, which will specify anywhere between 3 and 27 `_partial_fit` calls.

This could be avoided if there's some way to specify the different Hyperband loops as independent, at least for synchronous Hyperband. This could work for asynchronous Hyperband too, as long as additional arguments are passed.

> run it [update] only after all of the previous instructions finish.

I think this should be supported as well. That's the simplest interface I would expect.

[1]:https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Online_machine_learning#Online_convex_optimisation

Note that in the early versions of SoS when all parameters are defined in a separate section, we include comments before parameter definition as comments for the parameter. This is deprecated because it can be confused with section comment, which we still honor. E.g.

```
[10]
# this steps does this
parameter a = 1
```
We could enforce something like

```
[10]
# this is section comment
parameter a = 1    # for whatever purpose
```
if it is helpful.

I really like the idea of a live_monitoring module. This printer function is also a great idea, but currently I think it's a bit too easy to screw up; any additional printing will interfere badly with it.

MWE of screwing it up:

```python
import numpy as np
from qcodes import new_experiment
from qcodes.dataset.data_set import new_data_set, ParamSpec
from qcodes.dataset.live_monitor import printer
from time import sleep
    
specs=[ParamSpec("x", "numeric", unit='Hz'), ParamSpec("y", "numeric", unit='V')]
new_experiment('test', 'no_sample')
data_set = new_data_set("test", specs=specs)
sub_id = data_set.subscribe(printer(["x", "y"]), state=[])
for x in np.linspace(100, 200, 15):
    y = np.random.randn() 
    data_set.add_result({"x": x, "y": y})
    sleep(0.2)
    print("I am here to wreck havoc")
```

That issue was about doing `a+=b` where `a` comes from Ray. It's expected to be slow because a is read-only so an extra copy is required.

This issue is about it being slow to load `a` onto GPU.  Both PyTorch and TensorFlow use `cudaMemCopyAsync` to copy things onto GPU, perhaps `cudaMemCopyAsync` has a problem with the kind of memory Ray provides

My sense is that __E901,E999,F821,F822,F823__ are the "showstopper" flake8 issues that can halt the runtime with a SyntaxError, NameError, etc. The other flake8 issues are merely "style violations" -- useful for readability but they do not effect runtime safety.

I would suggest keeping the --exclude list smaller but putting __#noqa__ on the lines where flake8 is doing the wrong thing.
